refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated diagnostics and alert propagation to enable rapid human intervention.
DP7: Implement configuration management and automated recovery procedures (restart, reset, reconfiguration) to restore normal operation without manual intervention.
DP8: Design system with built-in self-test and health-check mechanisms at multiple levels (component, subsystem, system) to continuously validate operational state.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP6 + DP7 (integrated approach): Achieve availability through redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, predictive maintenance to reduce fault occurrence, comprehensive fault detection and isolation with automated recovery procedures, and continuous health monitoring. This combination addresses both fault prevention (DP3) and fault tolerance (DP1, DP6, DP7) across the availability lifecycle.

potential_sources: DP1 (redundancy) and DP3 (predictive maintenance) are loosely coupled: redundancy masks faults while predictive maintenance prevents them—both are necessary but operate on different timescales. Risk: over-reliance on redundancy may reduce incentive to invest in predictive maintenance.

DP6 (FDIA) and DP7 (automated recovery) are tightly coupled by design: FDIA must feed diagnostic results to recovery procedures. This is acceptable coupling (functional dependency) but requires clear interface definition.
DP1 and DP7 interact: automated recovery procedures must account for redundancy topology to avoid cascading failures during failover. Requires careful state management.
DP3 (predictive maintenance) depends on external execution (human or external system) to act on predictions—introduces dependency on operational processes outside the NGHE system boundary.
coupling_check.notes: The selected combination exhibits functional coupling (expected and necessary) rather than structural coupling. The main risk is process-level coupling: predictive maintenance effectiveness depends on external maintenance scheduling and execution, which is outside direct system control. Recommend explicit interface definition between FDIA outputs and recovery automation to minimize latency.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be designed to fail independently (no common-mode failures). RISK: Environmental stressors (temperature, vibration, contamination) may cause correlated failures across redundant units. Mitigation: diversify redundancy (different technologies/suppliers) and environmental isolation.

ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance models are sufficiently accurate to detect degradation before failure. RISK: Model accuracy depends on historical data quality and applicability to field conditions. Mitigation: continuous model refinement and conservative alert thresholds.

ASSUMPTION: Automated recovery procedures are reliable and do not introduce new failure modes. RISK: Recovery logic itself may fail or cause unintended state transitions. Mitigation: extensive testing, watchdog timers, and manual override capability.

ASSUMPTION: Fault detection latency is acceptable (system can tolerate detection delay before failover). RISK: If detection is slow, transient faults may cause brief unavailability. Mitigation: define maximum acceptable detection latency and validate against acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Availability target of ≥0.97 is achievable with proposed DPs given system complexity and field environment. RISK: Achieving 97% availability (≤262 hours downtime/year) may require very high redundancy and maintenance overhead. Mitigation: validate cost-benefit trade-off and confirm customer acceptance of operational burden.

ASSUMPTION: External maintenance processes (scheduling, execution, parts availability) are reliable and responsive. RISK: Predictive maintenance effectiveness is limited by maintenance logistics. Mitigation: establish SLAs for maintenance response and parts availability.

ASSUMPTION: System can distinguish between transient faults (self-healing) and persistent faults (requiring intervention). RISK: Misclassification may trigger unnecessary failovers or miss real failures. Mitigation: implement multi-level confirmation logic before failover.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold (≥0.97 over the agreed evaluation period) through a combination of: (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover to mask single-point failures, (2) predictive maintenance to reduce fault occurrence, (3) comprehensive fault detection and isolation with automated recovery procedures, and (4) continuous health monitoring. The solution shall define clear interfaces between fault detection outputs and recovery automation, establish maximum acceptable detection latency, and document assumptions regarding independent failure modes, maintenance process reliability, and model accuracy. Acceptance criteria shall include both availability measurement (≥0.97) and validation of fault detection latency, recovery success rate, and predictive maintenance accuracy over the evaluation period.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for fault detection, isolation, health monitoring, predictive maintenance coordination, and automated recovery procedures across all vehicle subsystems and teleoperation interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives continuous health and operational data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems for monitoring and predictive analysis.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits fault detection results, isolation decisions, and recovery commands to Vehicle and Teleoperation for automated failover and corrective actions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational task context from NGHE Solution; returns availability status and maintenance recommendations to inform task scheduling and resource allocation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicates with remote monitoring and predictive maintenance services; receives external health analytics and sends diagnostic telemetry for off-board analysis.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Safety Services is the natural home for fault detection, isolation, health monitoring, and automated recovery. Comprehensive fault detection (DP6) and continuous health monitoring (DP7) are core safety functions. Predictive maintenance coordination (DP3) aligns with safety-critical lifecycle management. Redundancy failover logic (DP1) can be orchestrated here with minimal diffusion to Vehicle/Teleoperation (only command/status interfaces needed).

Poor fit. While Vehicle contains physical redundancy hardware, centralizing fault detection, health monitoring, and recovery logic here would scatter safety-critical decision-making across subsystems. Predictive maintenance and cross-subsystem failover coordination would diffuse heavily to Teleoperation and NGHE Solution. Violates separation of concerns.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a user interface and command channel, not a safety orchestration layer. Embedding fault detection, health monitoring, and automated recovery here would couple safety logic to operator interaction, reducing autonomy and creating tight coupling with Vehicle subsystems.

Weak fit. NGHE Solution is a higher-level mission/task orchestrator. While it may consume availability status and maintenance recommendations, embedding fault detection, isolation, and automated recovery logic here would create excessive coupling to low-level subsystem health and introduce latency in time-critical failover decisions.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the single authoritative module for the integrated availability strategy (DP1+DP3+DP6+DP7). It owns fault detection and isolation logic, health monitoring algorithms, predictive maintenance coordination, and automated recovery orchestration. Vehicle and Teleoperation remain passive responders to SafetyAlert commands; NGHE Solution consumes availability status via Tasking to inform mission planning. This architecture preserves safety-critical autonomy, minimizes diffusion, and maintains clear ownership boundaries.
containment_risks: Risk: Predictive maintenance (DP3) may require deep domain knowledge of Vehicle subsystem degradation patterns; Safety Services must have access to detailed telemetry and historical data to build accurate models. Mitigation: Establish clear Telemetry schema and data governance; consider off-board analytics via Network Link if on-board compute is constrained.

Risk: Automated recovery procedures (DP6, DP7) must execute with minimal latency; Safety Services must not become a bottleneck. Mitigation: Design Safety Services as a real-time, event-driven module with pre-computed recovery policies; avoid synchronous blocking calls to Vehicle or Teleoperation.

Risk: Redundancy failover (DP1) may require Vehicle to expose multiple redundant subsystem interfaces; Safety Services must orchestrate selection. Mitigation: Define a clear redundancy abstraction in Vehicle (e.g., primary/backup subsystem identifiers) and expose via SafetyAlert command protocol.

Risk: Cross-system health correlation (e.g., Vehicle + Teleoperation availability) may require Safety Services to reason about dependencies not fully visible in Telemetry. Mitigation: Establish explicit dependency declarations in system configuration; Safety Services must maintain a dependency model.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability target (≥0.97) is measurable and traceable to Safety Services module outputs (SafetyAlert commands, automated recovery execution, health monitoring telemetry). Verification can be conducted via: (1) fault injection testing to validate detection/isolation/recovery latency, (2) continuous monitoring of availability metrics via Telemetry and Network Link interfaces, (3) predictive maintenance effectiveness tracking via historical degradation data. Clear acceptance criteria exist; Safety Services must demonstrate zero undetected critical faults and recovery time within acceptable bounds.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Safety Services owns the integrated DP1+DP3+DP6+DP7 strategy with well-defined external interfaces (Telemetry in, SafetyAlert out, Tasking inout, Network Link inout). Vehicle and Teleoperation are passive responders; NGHE Solution consumes availability status for mission planning. Ownership boundary is unambiguous; no competing module claims responsibility for fault detection, health monitoring, or automated recovery.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP (redundancy + predictive maintenance + fault detection/isolation + health monitoring) directly and sufficiently addresses FR (operational availability ≥0.97). The integrated approach covers both fault prevention (DP3 predictive maintenance reduces fault occurrence) and fault tolerance (DP1, DP6, DP7 enable rapid detection and recovery). No logical gaps; the combination is necessary and sufficient to achieve the availability target over the evaluation period.

diffusion_risks: DP3 (Predictive Maintenance): Risk of diffusion if Vehicle subsystem degradation models are not centralized. Mitigation: Safety Services must own the predictive analytics engine and receive comprehensive Telemetry schema from Vehicle; consider off-board analytics via Network Link if on-board compute is constrained. Verify that predictive maintenance logic is not duplicated across Vehicle or Teleoperation modules.

DP6 + DP7 (Fault Detection/Isolation + Health Monitoring): Risk of latency bottleneck if Safety Services becomes synchronous orchestrator. Mitigation: Design as event-driven, real-time module with pre-computed recovery policies; avoid blocking calls. Verify response time to critical faults is within acceptable bounds (recommend <100ms for safety-critical failover).

DP1 (Redundancy Failover): Risk of diffusion if Vehicle does not expose clear redundancy abstraction (primary/backup subsystem identifiers). Mitigation: Establish explicit redundancy interface contract in Vehicle; Safety Services must orchestrate selection via SafetyAlert commands. Verify that failover logic is not scattered across Vehicle and Teleoperation.

Cross-system Health Correlation: Risk of incomplete visibility if Safety Services cannot reason about Vehicle + Teleoperation dependencies. Mitigation: Establish explicit dependency declarations in system configuration; Safety Services must maintain and update a dependency model. Verify that all critical dependencies are declared and monitored.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. This is excellent fit. Fault detection (DP6), health monitoring (DP7), and predictive maintenance coordination (DP3) are core safety functions naturally owned by Safety Services. Redundancy failover orchestration (DP1) can be centralized here with minimal diffusion—only command/status interfaces to Vehicle and Teleoperation are required. Vehicle and Teleoperation remain passive responders; NGHE Solution consumes availability status via Tasking interface. No competing module claims responsibility. Recommended action: Proceed with Safety Services as single authoritative owner; establish clear Telemetry schema, SafetyAlert command protocol, and dependency model to mitigate identified risks.

recommended_revisions: Establish and document Telemetry schema: Define all health and operational data fields that Vehicle and Teleoperation must transmit to Safety Services. Include subsystem state, fault codes, performance metrics, and historical degradation indicators. Ensure schema supports predictive maintenance analytics.

Define SafetyAlert command protocol: Specify the format and semantics of fault detection results, isolation decisions, and automated recovery commands. Include failover directives (primary/backup subsystem selection), corrective actions, and priority levels. Ensure Vehicle and Teleoperation can execute commands with <100ms latency for safety-critical faults.

Formalize redundancy abstraction in Vehicle: Expose primary/backup subsystem identifiers and health status via SafetyAlert interface. Define failover logic (e.g., automatic switchover on primary failure, graceful degradation). Verify that Safety Services can orchestrate failover without deep coupling to Vehicle internals.

Establish dependency model: Document all critical dependencies between Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems (e.g., Teleoperation depends on Vehicle power, Vehicle depends on Teleoperation command link). Safety Services must maintain and monitor this model to enable cross-system health correlation.

Define off-board analytics interface via Network Link: If on-board compute is constrained, specify how Safety Services sends diagnostic telemetry to remote monitoring services and receives predictive maintenance recommendations. Ensure latency and bandwidth constraints are acceptable for real-time failover decisions.

Specify acceptance criteria for Safety Services module: Define measurable requirements for fault detection latency, recovery time, predictive maintenance accuracy, and health monitoring coverage. Tie to FR acceptance criterion (≥0.97 availability). Include fault injection test plan and continuous monitoring metrics.

Document risk mitigations: Formalize mitigations for latency bottleneck, incomplete visibility, and compute constraints. Include design patterns (e.g., event-driven architecture, pre-computed recovery policies, caching of dependency model) and verification methods.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM alignment with high confidence (all three gates pass). However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal that Safety Services' internal decomposition requires explicit sub-level FRs to operationalize the integrated DP strategy. Specifically: (1) Telemetry schema definition, SafetyAlert protocol, and redundancy abstraction are architectural contracts that must be derived as child FRs to ensure Safety Services can execute DP1+DP3+DP6+DP7 without latency bottlenecks or diffusion. (2) High safety criticality demands rigorous decomposition of fault detection (DP6), health monitoring (DP7), and predictive maintenance (DP3) into verifiable sub-requirements with explicit latency and accuracy targets. (3) Cross-system dependency modeling (DP7 extension) requires a dedicated sub-FR to formalize visibility and correlation logic. Proceeding directly to implementation without this decomposition risks: latency violations in failover (<100ms requirement), incomplete health correlation, and diffusion of predictive maintenance logic across Vehicle/Teleoperation. Zigzag down to derive child FRs that operationalize the Safety Services module design.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Ingest and normalize Vehicle and Teleoperation health telemetry (subsystem state, fault codes, performance metrics, degradation indicators) via standardized Telemetry schema with <50ms latency to enable real-time fault detection and predictive analytics.
FR1.2: Detect critical faults in Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems with zero false negatives and <100ms detection-to-alert latency to enable automated failover and operator notification.

FR1.3: Isolate detected faults to specific subsystems and root causes, and command appropriate recovery actions (failover, corrective procedures, graceful degradation) via SafetyAlert protocol with <100ms command execution latency.

FR1.4: Monitor continuous health status of Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, correlate cross-system dependencies, and maintain real-time availability state (≥0.97 target) for mission planning and operator awareness.

FR1.5: Predict subsystem degradation and maintenance needs using Vehicle telemetry history and predictive models, and recommend preventive actions to reduce fault occurrence and extend operational availability.

FR1.6: Formalize and maintain a dependency model of critical Vehicle-Teleoperation interactions (e.g., power, command link, sensor data) to enable Safety Services to reason about cascading failures and cross-system health correlation.

FR1.7: Expose redundancy abstraction (primary/backup subsystem identifiers, health status, failover policies) via Vehicle interface to enable Safety Services to orchestrate failover without deep coupling to Vehicle internals.

revise.why: Current DP (DP1+DP3+DP6+DP7 integrated strategy) is logically sound but requires explicit decomposition into child DPs that operationalize each sub-FR. For example: DP1.1 (Telemetry ingestion pipeline with schema validation and latency monitoring), DP1.2 (Real-time fault detection engine with pre-computed decision trees and <100ms SLA), DP1.3 (Event-driven recovery orchestrator with pre-computed failover policies), DP1.4 (Health correlation engine with dependency model), DP1.5 (Predictive maintenance analytics with off-board option via Network Link), DP1.6 (Dependency model repository and monitoring), DP1.7 (Redundancy abstraction adapter). This decomposition ensures that architectural contracts (Telemetry schema, SafetyAlert protocol, redundancy interface) are explicit design decisions, not implicit assumptions, and that latency, accuracy, and diffusion risks are addressed at the appropriate level of detail.

assumptions: Safety Services can achieve <100ms fault detection and recovery latency with event-driven, real-time architecture (not synchronous orchestration).
Vehicle subsystem exposes clear redundancy abstraction (primary/backup identifiers) and comprehensive Telemetry schema; Teleoperation provides equivalent health data.
Predictive maintenance analytics can be centralized in Safety Services (or off-board via Network Link) without duplicating logic across Vehicle/Teleoperation.
Cross-system dependencies (Vehicle-Teleoperation interactions) can be formally declared and monitored without requiring deep coupling between modules.
Availability target (≥0.97) is achievable with the integrated DP strategy over the evaluation period; no fundamental architectural constraints prevent this target.
open_risks: Latency bottleneck: If Safety Services becomes synchronous orchestrator or if Telemetry ingestion pipeline has >50ms latency, fault detection and failover may exceed <100ms SLA. Mitigation: Design as event-driven, real-time module with pre-computed recovery policies; measure and validate latency in fault injection tests.

Incomplete health visibility: If Vehicle or Teleoperation does not expose comprehensive Telemetry schema or if critical dependencies are not declared, Safety Services cannot reason about cross-system failures. Mitigation: Establish explicit Telemetry schema and dependency model; verify coverage via requirements traceability.

Diffusion of predictive maintenance: If Vehicle subsystem maintains its own degradation models or if Teleoperation performs independent health analytics, predictive maintenance logic will be scattered. Mitigation: Centralize predictive analytics in Safety Services; establish clear ownership boundary and interface contract.

Redundancy abstraction coupling: If Vehicle does not expose clear primary/backup abstraction, Safety Services may become tightly coupled to Vehicle internals. Mitigation: Formalize redundancy interface contract; verify that failover logic is not scattered across modules.

Compute constraints: If on-board compute is insufficient for real-time predictive analytics, Safety Services may require off-board analytics via Network Link, introducing latency and dependency on external services. Mitigation: Define off-board analytics interface; measure latency impact on failover decisions.
evidence_needed: Telemetry schema specification: Document all health and operational data fields, data types, update frequencies, and latency requirements. Verify that schema supports fault detection, health monitoring, and predictive maintenance analytics.

SafetyAlert command protocol specification: Define format, semantics, and latency SLAs for fault detection results, isolation decisions, and recovery commands. Include failover directives, corrective actions, and priority levels.

Redundancy abstraction interface contract: Specify primary/backup subsystem identifiers, health status fields, and failover logic. Verify that Safety Services can orchestrate failover via SafetyAlert commands without deep coupling to Vehicle internals.
Dependency model formalization: Document all critical Vehicle-Teleoperation dependencies (e.g., power, command link, sensor data). Specify how Safety Services maintains and monitors this model.

Latency measurement plan: Define how to measure fault detection latency, recovery time, and Telemetry ingestion latency. Specify acceptance criteria (<100ms for safety-critical failover, <50ms for Telemetry ingestion).

Fault injection test plan: Define test scenarios for critical faults (e.g., primary subsystem failure, Teleoperation link loss, Vehicle sensor degradation). Verify that Safety Services detects faults with zero false negatives and executes recovery within latency SLA.
Predictive maintenance validation: Define how to measure predictive maintenance accuracy (e.g., precision, recall, false positive rate). Specify acceptance criteria and continuous monitoring metrics.

Off-board analytics interface specification (if applicable): Define how Safety Services sends diagnostic telemetry to remote services and receives recommendations. Measure latency and bandwidth impact on failover decisions.
